query: Does rotavirus undergo genetic reassortment during co-infection?

retrieved_docs.title: Rotavirus -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Rotavirus is the leading cause of acute gastroenteritis in the world. Studies estimate that approximately 200,000 people die annually from infection. Although the infection rate has decreased with the advent of vaccines, infections are still common throughout the world. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of rotavirus infection and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in evaluating and treating patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Rotavirus -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Lab testing is generally not performed, but it is the only way to confirm the diagnosis of rotavirus. In severe, intractable cases of infection, a confirmatory lab test may be indicated to solidify the diagnosis of rotavirus. [3] When a laboratory-confirmed diagnosis is desired, a rotavirus antigen can be found in stool samples using enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay or immunochromatography. The addition of reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction assays is more sensitive and allows genotyping of virus isolates and as such, may be indicated in epidemiological studies. [1] Additional methods of detection include electron microscopy, polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis, antigen detection assays, and virus isolation. [3] In most cases, confirmation testing is only indicated if it could potentially cut costs by decreasing hospital stays or avoiding unnecessary procedures. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Rotavirus -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Due to the broad differential diagnosis for acute gastroenteritis, accurate diagnosis of rotavirus infection requires strong interprofessional communication between the patient, family, nurses, emergency room physicians, primary care physicians, infectious disease physicians, and pediatricians. Transmission of rotavirus occurs primarily through the fecal-oral route and is frequently passed between individuals in daycare or household settings. Once an accurate diagnosis is made, the whole interprofessional team must educate the patient and the family on the importance of handwashing and disinfection techniques to prevent the transmission cycle of the virus. Patients should be advised to quarantine and stay home from school until complete symptom resolution.

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Gastroenteritis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: This course explores the various aspects of viral gastroenteritis, including the differential diagnoses that should be considered and common etiologic pathogens. The introduction of vaccines, particularly for rotavirus, has significantly reduced the disease burden in many countries, yet viral gastroenteritis continues to contribute to global childhood morbidity and mortality. Treatment focuses on symptomatic relief and hydration to prevent complications. Therefore, the ability of each healthcare team member to collaborate in the clinical recognition and management of this frequently encountered condition is critical. This activity for healthcare professionals is designed to enhance the learner's competence in identifying viral gastroenteritis, performing the recommended evaluation, and implementing an appropriate interprofessional approach when managing this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Diarrhea -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Other significant virus outbreaks may occur in schools, cruise ships, and other highly populated areas. Rotavirus, of the Reoviridae family, is a nonenveloped and double-stranded RNA virus. On the other hand, enteric adenovirus is a nonenveloped and double-stranded DNA virus, with types 40 and 41 primarily responsible for causing gastroenteritis, commonly observed in children younger than 2. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Rotavirus -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Ten different species of rotavirus are currently classified (A-J). Species A rotaviruses are the most common cause of childhood infections, with species B and C also causing a smaller but significant percentage of infections across the world. [2] [3] Geographical variances in the strains of species A rotaviruses have been seen circulating globally. [1] Rotavirus disease epidemiology has drastically changed since the development of vaccines against the virus. Prior to vaccine development, rotavirus infection was most common in children younger than 5. Following widespread vaccination programs, rotavirus appears to be more prevalent in older, unvaccinated children. In low-income countries where rotavirus vaccines are not widespread, the prevalence of rotavirus infections appears to be stable. Malnutrition in these regions tends to increase the severity of disease. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Rotavirus -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Rotavirus is a double-stranded ribonucleic acid virus, named for its classic “wheel-shaped” appearance on electron microscopy (see Image. Rotavirus). [1] Transmission of rotavirus primarily occurs through the fecal-oral route. Additionally, the viral spread can occur through contaminated hands, fomites, and, rarely, food and water. [3] Prior to the routine use of the rotavirus vaccines, the rates of rotavirus illness among children in low-income countries were similar to those of high-income countries. [2] Since the advent of rotavirus vaccines, norovirus has exceeded rotavirus as the leading cause of acute viral gastroenteritis in high-income countries. In low-income countries and globally, rotavirus infections are the number one cause of acute viral gastroenteritis in children.

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Gastroenteritis -- Etiology -- Rotavirus
retrieved_docs.content: Infants and young children who develop severe dehydration are more likely to have an infection from rotavirus than other viral gastroenteritis pathogens. Viral shedding of infectious particles can occur in the stool for up to 10 days. [6] Adults are more likely to develop an asymptomatic infection with a rise in antibody titer. Immunosuppressed individuals can experience more prolonged and severe disease, with longer viral shedding. [7] Rotavirus pathogenesis is complicated, with several possible mechanisms, including malabsorption from mucosal damage, viral enterotoxin secretion, and enteric secretions in response to the virus. Rotavirus increases electrolyte secretion from the small intestine and decreases the glucose cotransport of these electrolytes. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Rotavirus -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Review the pathophysiology of rotavirus. Outline the evaluation of a patient with rotavirus. Explain the treatment of patients with rotavirus. Summarize how communication between the patient, family, nurses, emergency room physicians, primary care physicians, infectious disease physicians, and pediatricians is required to decrease the incidence as well as morbidity and mortality associated with the infection. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Enterovirus -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Asymptomatic viral presence in test samples has been documented in varying frequencies. Based on the clinical concern, providers should attempt to rule out other forms of infection/co-infection by bacteria, fungi, and other viruses.

retrieved_docs.title: Enterovirus -- Epidemiology -- Rhinovirus Species
retrieved_docs.content: Vaccine development is limited by the limited cross-reactivity between the expansive numbers of enteroviruses, precluding the development of polyvalent vaccines. Further difficulties arise from uncertain waxing and waning of vaccine-derived immunity, as seen in the poliovirus vaccine. [2] [3] [4] [6] [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Gastroenteritis -- Etiology -- Rotavirus
retrieved_docs.content: Before vaccination, the United States saw an estimated 55,000 to 70,000 hospitalizations of children younger than 5 due to rotavirus. According to the CDC, this number has decreased by 40,000 to 50,000 since the vaccine became available. Although the incidence of rotavirus in the United States has been historically seasonal, with peak seasonality being from December to April, this pattern has become very inconsistent since vaccination became common. [13] Now, the virus tends to infect individuals in the United States sporadically throughout the year. Despite the widespread use of the vaccine in developed countries, rotavirus is still the leading cause of infantile diarrheal illness worldwide, with the CDC estimating that 215,000 rotavirus-related deaths occurred in 2013. Although there has been a continued decline in disease burden, some regions of the world are still disproportionately affected by this virus. [14] More than 40% of World Health Organization member countries have initiated large-scale vaccination of children. This number is anticipated to rise in the next few years. In turn, infection and mortality caused by rotavirus are expected to continue declining. Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Rotavirus ", for further information.

retrieved_docs.title: Enterovirus -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The development of vaccines against enteroviruses is hindered by the large number of distinct viruses, limited cross-reactivity, and difficulty predicting epidemiological patterns specific to serotypes. To date, only the poliovirus has an effective vaccine. Maintenance of adequate titers requires repeated boosters.

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Gastroenteritis -- Etiology -- Rotavirus
retrieved_docs.content: Throughout recorded history, rotavirus has been the leading cause of episodic infantile illness worldwide. However, in 2006, an oral vaccine was introduced. Since the introduction and utilization of this vaccine, the United States and many other industrialized countries have seen a sharp decline in the number and severity of gastroenteritis cases caused by rotavirus. Before 2006, studies estimated that over 3.5 million infants were affected annually in the United States and that rotavirus led to 440,000 deaths annually worldwide in children younger than 5. [9] Since the routine vaccination of children, each year has seen a significant reduction in cases. [10] [11] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 576
retrieved_docs.content: Abacavir / lamivudine is a combination antiviral medication that is classified as a member of the group “Antivirals for treatment of HIV infections, combinations.”

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 2579
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors, direct acting antivirals constitute a therapeutic class that includes the antiviral agents fosamprenavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir. While all three drugs share the common mechanism of inhibiting viral proteases, atazanavir carries a specific contraindication: it should not be co‑administered with the chemotherapy agent irinotecan because of a known drug‑interaction risk. Recognizing that fosamprenavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir belong to the same protease‑inhibitor class, and that atazanavir is contraindicated with irinotecan, is essential for safe prescribing and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1227
retrieved_docs.content: Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors constitute a class of antiretroviral drugs that target the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme, thereby blocking viral replication. This therapeutic class includes tenofovir disoproxil, emtricitabine, lamivudine, zidovudine, and dideoxycytidine (also known as zalcitabine). By sharing a common mechanism of action, these agents are frequently combined in HIV treatment regimens to achieve synergistic viral suppression and reduce the risk of resistance development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 359
retrieved_docs.content: Remdesivir is an antiviral medication that may be used to treat a viral infection (عدوى فيروسية). This therapeutic relationship highlights Remdesivir’s potential role in managing viral diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 62
retrieved_docs.content: the importance of recognizing contraindicated drug classes, and the role of antiviral agents such as zanamivir in preventing viral respiratory illnesses that can lead to secondary bacterial or fungal pneumonias.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1732
retrieved_docs.content: Fosamprenavir, amprenavir, and tipranavir are all classified as protease inhibitors that belong to the group of direct‑acting antiviral agents. As protease inhibitors, each drug specifically targets viral protease enzymes, a critical step in the replication cycle of viruses such as HIV, thereby providing a mechanism of action that directly interferes with viral maturation. Their shared categorization as direct‑acting antivirals highlights that they act on viral components rather than host cellular processes, which informs clinicians about common therapeutic objectives, potential for cross‑resistance, and similar considerations regarding drug‑drug interactions and adverse effect profiles in antiretroviral treatment regimens.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1431
retrieved_docs.content: Influenza, referred to in Arabic as الالتهاب الرئوي, is a viral respiratory infection that can lead to or be associated with pneumonia. The broader term الالتهاب الرئوي also includes specific forms such as التهاب رئوي وليدي, which denotes neonatal pneumonia, a distinct subtype of pneumonia occurring in newborn infants. Understanding that influenza may precipitate pneumonia and that neonatal pneumonia is a particular manifestation of pneumonia aids clinicians in identifying at‑risk patients, especially infants, and in applying appropriate preventive and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1231
retrieved_docs.content: Abacavir, an antiretroviral nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor used in HIV treatment, is mapped to dideoxyadenosine, indicating a direct chemical or functional correspondence between the two nucleoside analogues. This mapping highlights the structural similarity of abacavir to dideoxyadenosine, which can be relevant for understanding its mechanism of action, potential cross‑reactivity, and the design of related therapeutic agents.
